actionable_feedback: Provide an explicit numeric threshold (or an explicit, reproducible procedure for selecting it) in the decision_table. Show the mapping from score -> FP/FN counts -> weighted_cost so reviewers can confirm the choice without manual interpretation.
Include a machine-readable export of workbook sheets (CSV or JSON) alongside the .xlsx so automated verifiers can inspect cells even if openpyxl is unavailable.

Add visible algebraic formula rows in the calculations sheet that show substitutions used (e.g., false_accept_cost = 5 * false_reject_cost) and intermediate arithmetic so human verifiers can trace computations directly in the workbook.
Add a calibration/threshold-distribution sheet (score histogram or calibration curve) that shows how thresholds map to FP/FN rates and supports the recommended cutoff selection.

Expand the risk notes to state operational impacts and explicit business tolerances (e.g., maximum acceptable false-accept rate) that justify the chosen threshold. If a numeric business maximum exists, reference it explicitly in the decision justification.
Ensure citation mapping and any provenance cells are on a visible sheet (not hidden or in cell comments) so automated and human verifiers can see data sources.
artifact_specific_comments: Artifact filename and presence are correct. Verifier could not parse the workbook programmatically in this environment; adding CSV/JSON exports will resolve automated parsing issues.
Calculations appear correct when recomputed from the normalized actual_outputs; include visible formula rows for stronger evidence.
feedback_summary: Submission meets the artifact contract and numeric recomputations are algebraically consistent, but verification could not parse the .xlsx programmatically (openpyxl unavailable) and the deliverable omits an explicit, actionable numeric decision cutoff. These two issues reduce verifiability and operational actionability.
